summary: Uncle Bob recounts his shock upon learning from astronomer Dr. Pamela Gay's podcast that sexual harassment is a commonplace, everyday occurrence for women in science and tech fields. He conducted research and found extensive documentation of harassment in the software community, including at conferences he attended. He connects this behavior to the dramatic decline of women in programming since 1985, suggesting harassment may explain why women left the field in droves. The post concludes with an uncompromising professional standard: never touch anyone without explicit consent, always intervene when witnessing harassment, and terminate offenders immediately regardless of their perceived indispensability.
key_points: Sexual harassment in tech is not anomalous behavior by a few bad actors but a commonplace, regular occurrence that women have come to expect as normal
Women programmers started leaving the field dramatically around 1985, coinciding with the rise of 'brogrammer' culture and programmers gaining significant status and power
The demand for programmers and resulting perks have created a sense of impunity among some who believe their skills make them untouchable
Many harassment accounts have been voluntarily deleted by victims, suggesting the trauma is so significant they cannot bear to leave records intact
Professional programmers must never touch anyone without explicit consent, must intervene when witnessing harassment, and employers must terminate offenders regardless of their perceived value
money_quotes: I've always thought that sexual harassment was anomalous behavior perpetrated by a few disgusting, arrogant men in positions of power. It never occurred to me that sexual harassment was an everyday, commonplace, run-of-the-mill, what-else-is-new occurrence.
Who does something like that? What kind of low-life, scumbag, knuckle-dragging, troglodyte, behaves that way? Drunk or not? Didn't their fathers ever teach them about respect?
I can't imagine living like that – having to tolerate being manhandled – having to always be on guard against it.

No wonder women avoid the field! No wonder at all. I imagine that they find out, very early on, that to stay in this field is to have to submit to being regularly manhandled by too many of their co-workers and bosses.
Power has a gender, and that gender is male.
You never lay your hands on someone with sexual intent without their explicit permission. It does not matter how drunk you are.
No one is so indispensable that you should tolerate, or cover up, this kind of behavior.
If your employers do not act decisively in such matters, quit!
key_insight: Sexual harassment in tech is not a rare aberration but a systemic, everyday reality that has driven women from the field, and true professionalism demands zero tolerance and active intervention.
